title: Climate Justice
description: An examination of differentiated responsibility between nations in climate policy.
speaker: Narrator
text: Climate change is often discussed as a global challenge requiring global cooperation. But the distribution of its causes and its consequences is deeply unequal.
Historically, the countries that contributed most to accumulated greenhouse gas emissions are primarily wealthy, industrialised nations.
The countries most vulnerable to the consequences of climate change — sea level rise, extreme weather events, agricultural disruption — are often among the least responsible for causing them.

This asymmetry raises a fundamental question of justice. If the costs of a problem fall most heavily on those who contributed least to creating it, then addressing that problem ethically requires differentiated responsibility.
For wealthy nations, this means not only reducing their own emissions but financing the transition to clean energy in developing economies.
For developing nations, the challenge is pursuing growth in ways that avoid repeating the high-emission pathways of industrialisation.
Effective climate policy must recognise that justice and feasibility are not separate concerns.
An agreement that is technically ambitious but perceived as unfair will not generate the sustained cooperation that climate action requires.

questions.question: What asymmetry does the speaker highlight in climate change?
options: Those least responsible often face the greatest consequences
questions.correctAnswer: Those least responsible often face the greatest consequences

questions.question: What does differentiated responsibility require of wealthy nations?
options: Reducing emissions only within their borders
Financing clean energy transitions in developing economies
questions.correctAnswer: Financing clean energy transitions in developing economies

questions.question: What challenge do developing nations face specifically?
options: Pursuing growth without repeating high-emission pathways
questions.correctAnswer: Pursuing growth without repeating high-emission pathways

questions.question: Why must justice and feasibility be addressed together?
options: An unfair agreement will not sustain the cooperation needed
questions.correctAnswer: An unfair agreement will not sustain the cooperation needed
